_notes: Schisms (rejected alternatives) considered during the session. Each entry records the chosen path, the rejected path, and why. Preserves the decision trace for future similar incidents.

data.context: C0 v3 deploy procedure — how to clear Firestore state before reseeding
data.rejected: Option A: Delete household doc + all subcollections (feeds, sleep, poop, caretickets, notes, singles, invites, backups, tracking) via Firebase Console. Each subcollection requires manual clearing since Firestore doesn't cascade-delete.
data.chosen: Option B (refined, then executed): Architect deleted household via Firebase Console (he'd done it before reading the abandon-alternative). Net effect identical to Option A.
data.reason: Mobile Firebase Console makes ten+ delete operations tedious and misclick-prone. Initially proposed abandoning the household via single members-map edit, but Architect had already completed the full delete. Outcome: clean Firestore state achieved either way. Recorded for future reference: abandon-via-member-removal is a valid alternative when full delete is procedurally expensive.

data.context: Post-B3 analysis — how to prevent first-listener-fire wipe after C0 deploy
data.rejected: Fix 6: Add a reconcile-lite mechanism to C0 that detects 'remote shrunk relative to local-at-attach' on first listener fire and defers the save + triggers a flush. ~20 LOC but scope-creep on C0.
data.chosen: Option Y deploy procedure: delete household doc + let Architect's phone recreate fresh. Phone's local seeds to new Firestore. No listener-wipe because remote starts == local. Wife rejoins via new invite code; her listener pulls Architect's authoritative state.
data.reason: Fix 6 duplicates logic that belongs in Session 1 reconcile (spec §6 _localPending + union merge). Adding it to C0 would bloat the emergency patch and potentially conflict with the real reconcile when that ships. Cleaner: sidestep via deploy procedure. Spec §6 owns the real reconciliation semantics.

data.context: Maren BLOCKER-2 on C0 v3 initial design — Fix 1 empty-remote-overwrite guard
data.rejected: Blanket empty-guard: for ANY single-doc key, if remoteVal is empty array or empty object and local has data, skip the overwrite. Initial Lyra proposal.
data.chosen: ALWAYS_POPULATED_KEYS allowlist: guard fires only for keys where empty-state is never a valid user intent (doctors, foods, vacc, meds). Keys outside the allowlist (notes, activityLog, feeding, sleep, poop, visits, episodes) propagate user-initiated clear-all as legitimate sync events.
data.reason: Maren: 'If a parent clears all poop entries on Device A intending to reset, blanket-guard blocks the propagation. Parent A sees clean state, Parent B still sees old entries. Divergent household, parent cannot trust app to reflect their intent.' Per-key semantic policy preserves legitimate deletions while protecting keys that should never legitimately be empty.

data.context: Diagnosis of 'data disappearing every refresh' symptom — why Architect's UID seemed missing from household members
data.rejected: Hypothesis: Architect's primary phone was accidentally authenticated as his wife's Google account (via account-picker confusion), leading to wife's UID being used for sync queries and household-membership.
data.chosen: Simpler explanation: Architect misread the Firebase Console members map (mobile UI truncation made the UID hex display ambiguous). His phone had never been auth'd as his wife; his own UID was in the map all along.
data.reason: Architect: 'This is my mobile, I dont think my wifes id can become admin from this device as I dont have her id saved or login on my phone.' Correct. The identity-confusion hypothesis required multiple unusual invariants to flip (Google account-picker showing wife's account on his device, him accidentally selecting it, no subsequent re-auth correction). Simpler explanation (UI display artifact) required zero unusual invariants. Rule of thumb adopted: **when a symptom admits multiple explanations, rank them by how many invariants each requires to flip.**

data.context: Consul canon-scope judgment during C0 v3 review
data.rejected: Promote HR-14 (Reconcile Sync Invariant) from candidate to committed now, as part of C0 v3 ship.
data.chosen: Defer HR-14 promotion to post-Session-1 conventions pass.
data.reason: Consul: 'HR-14 is about the synchronous-region invariant inside reconcile. C0 v3 does not implement reconcile. Promoting HR-14 now would canonize a rule no code is yet subject to — premature. Keep it candidate. Lyra promotes in the post-Session-1 conventions pass when she has lived under the rule and can speak to whether it held in practice.' A canon that codifies behavior that doesn't exist in code is scaffolding, not doctrine.

data.context: Initial framing of C0 v3 — Lyra proposed shipping directly after self-analysis
data.rejected: Lyra ships C0 v3 directly after her own analysis, without formal Governor review, because 'the user is losing data in real-time and speed matters.'
data.chosen: Full four-governor review chain per Edict V: Maren → Kael → Cipher → Consul. Architect's correction: 'Why would we skip hierarchy? I have a backup, we want to get it right.'
data.reason: Data loss creates urgency, but urgency does not override governance. The Architect had a downloaded backup — the ground wasn't burning. Shipping without review risks a 'fix' that moves a loud bug to a quiet one (Maren's BLOCKER-1 warning, which was exactly right). Kael's subsequent B3 discovery confirmed the hierarchy earned its keep — without formal review, C0 v3 would have been cosmetic, missing the dominant bug. **Canon-candidate: do not trade governance for speed when a safety net exists.**
